steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses needing connection
steps.content: The sentence contains two complete thoughts: (1) “To some economists, whether a tax cut will ultimately increase Italy's tax revenue is dependent on the country's position on the Laffer [blank]”—a closed independent clause—and (2) “a theoretical relationship between tax rates and revenues, the curve was famously sketched on a napkin by economist Arthur Laffer in 1974”—another independent clause that must follow. Both are grammatical statements standing alone, so they require punctuation that properly joins independent clauses.

steps.title: Apply the semicolon rule for independent clauses
steps.content: A semicolon joins two independent clauses. Choice B correctly uses a semicolon after “curve.” In the second clause, “a theoretical relationship between tax rates and revenues” functions as an appositive renaming “the curve,” set off by commas: “[The curve,] a theoretical relationship between tax rates and revenues, was famously sketched…” This structure is grammatically sound and allows both thoughts to stand as a unified, punctuated sentence. Choices A and D lack proper punctuation or fail to separate the clauses correctly; choice C introduces a relative clause with “which is” but then presents “the curve was…” as an awkwardly disconnected independent clause. That matches choice B — B is correct.
